questions.question_text: A particle covers half of its total distance with speed $v_1$ and the rest half distance with speed $v_2$. Its average speed during the complete journey is:
questions.solution:
Let $S$ be the total distance travelled by the particle.

Time taken for first half:
$t_1 = \frac{S/2}{v_1} = \frac{S}{2v_1}$

Time taken for second half:
$t_2 = \frac{S/2}{v_2} = \frac{S}{2v_2}$

Average speed is given by:
$v_{av} = \frac{\text{Total distance}}{\text{Total time}}$

$v_{av} = \frac{S}{t_1 + t_2}$

$v_{av} = \frac{2v_1v_2}{v_1 + v_2}$

$\boxed{v_{av} = \frac{2v_1v_2}{v_1 + v_2}}$
questions.explanation: When distances are equal, average speed is the harmonic mean: $v_{av} = \frac{2v_1v_2}{v_1 + v_2}$

questions.question_text: A car moves from X to Y with a uniform speed $v_u$ and returns to X with a uniform speed $v_d$. The average speed for this round trip is:
questions.solution:
Let the distance between X and Y be $s$.

Time taken from X to Y:
$t_1 = \frac{s}{v_u}$

Time taken from Y to X:
$t_2 = \frac{s}{v_d}$

Total distance travelled:
$d = s + s = 2s$

Total time taken:
$t = t_1 + t_2 = \frac{s}{v_u} + \frac{s}{v_d}$

Average speed:
$v_{av} = \frac{2s}{\frac{s}{v_u} + \frac{s}{v_d}}$

$v_{av} = \frac{2v_u v_d}{v_u + v_d}$

$\boxed{v_{av} = \frac{2v_u v_d}{v_u + v_d}}$
questions.explanation: For equal distances, average speed is the harmonic mean: $v_{av} = \frac{2v_u v_d}{v_u + v_d}$

questions.question_text: A car runs at a constant speed on a circular track of radius 100 m, taking 62.8 seconds for every circular lap. The average velocity and average speed for each circular lap respectively is:
questions.solution:
Radius of track, $r = 100$ m

Time for one lap, $T = 62.8$ s

Distance travelled in one lap:
$d = 2\pi r = 2 \times 3.14 \times 100 = 628$ m

Average speed:
$v_{av} = \frac{d}{T} = \frac{628}{62.8} = 10$ m/s

Displacement in one lap:
$\Delta x = 0$ (returns to starting point)

Average velocity:
$\vec{v}_{av} = \frac{\Delta x}{T} = \frac{0}{62.8} = 0$

$\boxed{\text{Average velocity} = 0, \text{Average speed} = 10 \text{ m/s}}$
questions.explanation: Average speed depends on total distance, while average velocity depends on net displacement. In one complete circular lap, displacement is zero.

questions.question_text: A car moves a distance of 200 m. It covers the first half of the distance at speed 40 km/h and the second half of distance at speed $v$. The average speed is 48 km/h. The value of $v$ is:
questions.solution:
Total distance, $S = 200$ m $= 0.2$ km

First half distance, $S_1 = 100$ m $= 0.1$ km
Speed for first half, $v_1 = 40$ km/h

Second half distance, $S_2 = 100$ m $= 0.1$ km
Speed for second half, $v_2 = v$ km/h

Time for first half:
$t_1 = \frac{S_1}{v_1} = \frac{0.1}{40} = 0.0025$ h

Time for second half:
$t_2 = \frac{S_2}{v_2} = \frac{0.1}{v}$ h

Average speed:
$v_{av} = \frac{\text{Total distance}}{\text{Total time}}$

$48 = \frac{0.2}{0.0025 + \frac{0.1}{v}}$

$v = 60$

$\boxed{v = 60 \text{ km/h}}$
questions.explanation:
When distances are equal: $v_{av} = \frac{2v_1v_2}{v_1 + v_2}$

$48 = \frac{2 \times 40 \times v}{40 + v}$
$v = 60$ km/h

questions.question_text: A bus travelling the first one-third distance at a speed of 10 km/h, the next one-third at 20 km/h and the last one-third at 60 km/h. The average speed of the bus is:
questions.solution:
Let the total distance be $S$.

Time taken for first part:
$t_1 = \frac{S/3}{10} = \frac{S}{30}$

Time taken for second part:
$t_2 = \frac{S/3}{20} = \frac{S}{60}$

Time taken for third part:
$t_3 = \frac{S/3}{60} = \frac{S}{180}$

Total time:
$t = \frac{S}{30} + \frac{S}{60} + \frac{S}{180} = \frac{S}{18}$

Average speed:
$v_{av} = \frac{S}{S/18} = 18$

$\boxed{v_{av} = 18 \text{ km/h}}$
questions.explanation: When distances are equal, average speed is the harmonic mean.

questions.question_text: A car covers the first half of the distance between two places at 40 km/h and another half at 60 km/h. The average speed of the car is:
questions.solution:
Let the total distance be $s$.

Time taken for first half:
$t_1 = \frac{s/2}{40} = \frac{s}{80}$

Time taken for second half:
$t_2 = \frac{s/2}{60} = \frac{s}{120}$

Total time:
$t = \frac{s}{80} + \frac{s}{120} = \frac{s}{48}$

Average speed:
$v_{av} = \frac{s}{s/48} = 48$ km/h

$\boxed{v_{av} = 48 \text{ km/h}}$
questions.explanation: When distances are equal, average speed is the harmonic mean of the two speeds.

questions.question_text: Two cars P and Q start from a point at the same time in a straight line and their positions are represented by $x_P(t) = (at + bt^2)$ and $x_Q(t) = (ft - t^2)$. At what time do the cars have the same velocity?
questions.solution:
Position of car P: $x_P(t) = at + bt^2$
Velocity of car P: $v_P(t) = a + 2bt$

Position of car Q: $x_Q(t) = ft - t^2$
Velocity of car Q: $v_Q(t) = f - 2t$

For same velocity:
$a + 2bt = f - 2t$
$2t(b + 1) = f - a$
$t = \frac{f - a}{2(1 + b)}$

$\boxed{t = \frac{f - a}{2(1 + b)}}$
questions.explanation: Differentiate position to get velocity, then equate.

questions.question_text: If the velocity of a particle is $v = At + Bt^2$, where A and B are constants, then the distance travelled by it between 1 s and 2 s is:
questions.solution:
$v = \frac{ds}{dt} = At + Bt^2$

$s = \int (At + Bt^2) dt = \frac{At^2}{2} + \frac{Bt^3}{3} + C$

$s(1) = \frac{A}{2} + \frac{B}{3} + C$
$s(2) = 2A + \frac{8B}{3} + C$

$\Delta s = s(2) - s(1) = \frac{3A}{2} + \frac{7B}{3}$

$\boxed{\Delta s = \frac{3}{2}A + \frac{7}{3}B}$
questions.explanation: Integrate velocity to get displacement, then find difference between times.

questions.question_text: The displacement 'x' (in meter) of a particle of mass 'm' (in kg) moving in one dimension under the action of a force, is related to time 't' (in sec) by $t = \sqrt{x} + 3$. The displacement of the particle when its velocity is zero, will be:
questions.explanation: First express x as function of t, then differentiate to get velocity.

questions.question_text: A particle shows distance-time curve as given in this figure. The maximum instantaneous velocity of the particle is around the point:
questions.solution:
Instantaneous velocity is given by the slope of the distance-time curve:
$v = \frac{ds}{dt}$

The slope is highest at point C.

$\boxed{\text{Maximum velocity is at point C}}$
questions.explanation: On a distance-time graph, instantaneous velocity is the slope of the tangent at any point. Steeper slope means higher velocity.

questions.question_text: The position $x$ of a particle with respect to time $t$ along $x$-axis is given by $x = 9t^2 - t^3$ where $x$ is in metres and $t$ in seconds. What will be the position of this particle when it achieves maximum speed along the $+x$ direction?
questions.solution:
Given: $x = 9t^2 - t^3$

Speed: $v = \frac{dx}{dt} = 18t - 3t^2$

For maximum speed: $\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$

$18 - 6t = 0$

$t = 3$ s

Position at $t = 3$ s:
$x = 9(3)^2 - (3)^3 = 9 \times 9 - 27$

$x = 81 - 27 = 54$ m

$\boxed{x = 54 \text{ m}}$
questions.explanation: For maximum speed, differentiate velocity with respect to time and set to zero. Then find position at that time.

questions.question_text: A particle moves along a straight line OX. At a time $t$ (in seconds) the distance $x$ (in metres) of the particle from O is given by $x = 40 + 12t - t^3$. How long would the particle travel before coming to rest?
questions.solution:
Given: $x = 40 + 12t - t^3$

Velocity: $v = \frac{dx}{dt} = 12 - 3t^2$

Particle comes to rest when $v = 0$:

$12 - 3t^2 = 0$

$3t^2 = 12$

$t = 2$ s

Distance travelled before coming to rest:

$s = \int_0^2 v \, dt = \int_0^2 (12 - 3t^2) dt$

$s = \left[12t - t^3\right]_0^2$

$s = (24 - 8) - 0 = 16$ m

$\boxed{s = 16 \text{ m}}$
questions.explanation: First find time when velocity is zero by differentiating displacement. Then integrate velocity from 0 to that time to get distance.

questions.question_text: The displacement $x$ of a particle varies with time $t$ as $x = ae^{-\alpha t} + be^{\beta t}$, where $a, b, \alpha$ and $\beta$ are positive constants. The velocity of the particle will:
questions.solution:
Given: $x = ae^{-\alpha t} + be^{\beta t}$

Velocity: $v = \frac{dx}{dt}$

$v = -a\alpha e^{-\alpha t} + b\beta e^{\beta t}$

Since $b$, $\beta$ and $e^{\beta t}$ are all positive,

The term $b\beta e^{\beta t}$ continuously increases with time.

The term $-a\alpha e^{-\alpha t}$ becomes negligible as $t \to \infty$.

Therefore, velocity continuously increases with time.

$\boxed{\text{Velocity goes on increasing with time}}$
questions.explanation: The exponential term with positive exponent dominates at large times, causing velocity to increase continuously.

questions.question_text: The displacement-time graph of a moving particle is shown below. The instantaneous velocity of the particle is negative at the point:
questions.solution:
Instantaneous velocity is given by the slope of the displacement-time graph:

$v = \frac{ds}{dt}$

Velocity is negative when the slope of the graph is negative.

From the graph, the slope is negative at point E.

$\boxed{\text{Velocity is negative at point E}}$
questions.explanation: At point E, the graph has a negative slope, indicating the particle is moving in the negative direction.

questions.question_text: Which of the following curve does not represent motion in one dimension?
questions.solution:
In one-dimensional motion, the particle can have only one value of velocity at a given time.

Therefore, the position-time graph must be a single-valued function.

A curve that has two values of position for a single time does not represent one-dimensional motion.

$\boxed{\text{Option B does not represent motion in one dimension}}$
questions.explanation: In one-dimensional motion, the position-time graph must be single-valued (one position for each time). Option B has multiple positions for the same time.

questions.question_text: A particle of unit mass undergoes one-dimensional motion such that its velocity varies according to $v(x) = \beta x^{-2n}$, where $\beta$ and $n$ are constants and $x$ is the position of the particle. The acceleration of the particle as a function of $x$, is given by:
questions.solution:
Given: $v = \beta x^{-2n}$

For acceleration when velocity is a function of position, we use the formula $a = v \times \frac{dv}{dx}$

First, differentiate $v$ with respect to $x$:
$\frac{dv}{dx} = -2n\beta x^{-2n-1}$

Now multiply $v$ and $\frac{dv}{dx}$:
$a = (\beta x^{-2n}) \times (-2n\beta x^{-2n-1})$

Combine the terms:
$a = -2n\beta^2 x^{-2n-2n-1}$

$\boxed{a = -2n\beta^2 x^{-4n-1}}$

So, acceleration varies as $-2n\beta^2 x^{-4n-1}$.
questions.explanation: When velocity is given as a function of position, use the chain rule: $a = v \times \frac{dv}{dx}$. Then simplify by adding the powers of $x$.

questions.question_text: The motion of a particle along a straight line is described by equation $x = 8 + 12t - t^3$ where $x$ is in metre and $t$ in second. The retardation of the particle when its velocity becomes zero is:
questions.solution:
Given: $x = 8 + 12t - t^3$

Velocity is the rate of change of displacement:
$v = \frac{dx}{dt} = 12 - 3t^2$

When the particle comes to rest, $v = 0$:
$12 - 3t^2 = 0 \Rightarrow t = 2$ s

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity:
$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = -6t$

At $t = 2$ s, the acceleration is:
$a = -6 \times 2 = -12$ m/s$^2$

Retardation is simply the magnitude of negative acceleration, which is $12$ m/s$^2$.

$\boxed{\text{Retardation} = 12 \text{ m/s}^2}$
questions.explanation: Retardation means deceleration - it's the magnitude of negative acceleration. First find when velocity is zero, then find acceleration at that time.

questions.question_text: A particle moves a distance $x$ in time $t$ according to equation $x = (t + 5)^{-1}$. The acceleration of particle is proportional to:
questions.solution:
Given: $x = (t + 5)^{-1}$

Velocity is the rate of change of displacement:
$v = \frac{dx}{dt} = -(t + 5)^{-2}$

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity:
$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = 2(t + 5)^{-3}$

From the expression for velocity, we can write:
$(t + 5)^{-2} = -v$

We need $(t + 5)^{-3}$ for acceleration. Since $(t + 5)^{-2} = -v$,
raise both sides to the power $\frac{3}{2}$:
$(t + 5)^{-3} = (-v)^{3/2}$

Substitute this in acceleration:
$a = 2(-v)^{3/2}$

Therefore, $a \propto v^{3/2}$

$\boxed{\text{Acceleration is proportional to (velocity)}^{3/2}}$
questions.explanation: Find velocity and acceleration in terms of $t$, then eliminate $t$ to find the relationship between $a$ and $v$.

questions.question_text: A particle moving along x-axis has acceleration $f$, at time $t$, given by $f = f_0 \left(1 - \frac{t}{T}\right)$, where $f_0$ and $T$ are constants. The particle at $t = 0$ has zero velocity. In the time interval between $t = 0$ and the instant when $f = 0$, the particle's velocity $(v_x)$ is:
questions.solution:
Given: $f = f_0 \left(1 - \frac{t}{T}\right)$

First, find when acceleration becomes zero:
$f_0 \left(1 - \frac{t}{T}\right) = 0$
$1 - \frac{t}{T} = 0$
$t = T$

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity, so $f = \frac{dv}{dt}$.
Thus, $dv = f \, dt$.

Integrate from $t=0$ to $t=T$:
$\int_0^v dv = \int_0^T f_0 \left(1 - \frac{t}{T}\right) dt$

$v = f_0 \left[t - \frac{t^2}{2T}\right]_0^T$

$v = f_0 \left(T - \frac{T^2}{2T}\right)$

$v = f_0 \left(T - \frac{T}{2}\right)$

$v = \frac{1}{2} f_0 T$

$\boxed{v_x = \frac{1}{2} f_0 T}$
questions.explanation: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. Integrate acceleration from $t=0$ to $t=T$ to find the change in velocity. Since initial velocity is zero, the integral gives the final velocity.

questions.question_text: Motion of a particle is given by equation $s = (3t^3 + 7t^2 + 14t + 8)$ m. The value of acceleration of the particle at $t = 1$ sec is:
questions.solution:
Given: $s = 3t^3 + 7t^2 + 14t + 8$

Velocity is the rate of change of displacement:
$v = \frac{ds}{dt} = 9t^2 + 14t + 14$

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity:
$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = 18t + 14$

At $t = 1$ s:
$a = 18(1) + 14 = 32$ m/s$^2$

$\boxed{a = 32 \text{ m/s}^2}$
questions.explanation: Acceleration is the second derivative of displacement with respect to time. First differentiate to get velocity, then differentiate again to get acceleration.

questions.question_text: The position $x$ of a particle varies with time $(t)$ as $x = at^2 - bt^3$. The acceleration will be zero at time $t$ is equal to:
questions.solution:
Given: $x = at^2 - bt^3$

Velocity is the rate of change of position:
$v = \frac{dx}{dt} = 2at - 3bt^2$

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity:
$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = 2a - 6bt$

For acceleration to be zero:
$2a - 6bt = 0$

Solving for $t$:
$6bt = 2a$

$\boxed{t = \frac{a}{3b}}$
questions.explanation: Acceleration is zero when the rate of change of velocity is zero. First find velocity, then differentiate to get acceleration, and set it to zero.

questions.question_text: The acceleration of a particle is increasing linearly with time $t$ as $bt$. The particle starts from origin with an initial velocity $v_0$. The distance travelled by the particle in time $t$ will be:
questions.solution:
Given: Acceleration $a = bt$

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity, so $\frac{dv}{dt} = bt$

Integrating to find velocity:
$v = \int bt \, dt = \frac{bt^2}{2} + C$

At $t = 0$, $v = v_0$, so $C = v_0$

$v = \frac{bt^2}{2} + v_0$

Velocity is the rate of change of displacement, so $\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{bt^2}{2} + v_0$

Integrating to find displacement:
$x = \int \left(\frac{bt^2}{2} + v_0\right) dt$

$x = \frac{bt^3}{6} + v_0 t + C'$

At $t = 0$, $x = 0$, so $C' = 0$

$\boxed{x = v_0 t + \frac{1}{6} bt^3}$
questions.explanation: When acceleration is a function of time, we need to integrate twice: first to get velocity, then to get displacement. Use the initial conditions to find the constants of integration.

questions.question_text: A particle moves along a straight line such that its displacement at any time $t$ is given by $s = (t^3 - 6t^2 + 3t + 4)$ metres. The velocity when the acceleration is zero is:
questions.solution:
Given: $s = t^3 - 6t^2 + 3t + 4$

Velocity is the rate of change of displacement:
$v = \frac{ds}{dt} = 3t^2 - 12t + 3$

Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity:
$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = 6t - 12$

For acceleration to be zero:
$6t - 12 = 0$
$t = 2$ s

At $t = 2$ s, the velocity is:
$v = 3(2)^2 - 12(2) + 3$
$v = 12 - 24 + 3$

$\boxed{v = -9 \text{ m/s}}$
questions.explanation: First find the time when acceleration is zero by differentiating to get acceleration and setting it to zero. Then substitute that time in the velocity equation.

questions.question_text: A ball is thrown vertically downward with a velocity of 20 m/s from the top of a tower. It hits the ground after some time with a velocity of 80 m/s. The height of the tower is (take $g = 10$ m/s²):
questions.solution:
For a body moving with uniform acceleration, we use the equation $v^2 = u^2 + 2gh$.

Here, initial velocity $u = 20$ m/s, final velocity $v = 80$ m/s, and $g = 10$ m/s².

Substituting the values:
$80^2 = 20^2 + 2 \times 10 \times h$

$6400 = 400 + 20h$

$6000 = 20h$

$h = 300$ m

So, the height of the tower is 300 m.
questions.explanation: Use the third equation of motion $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ where $s$ is the height.

questions.question_text: A stone falls freely under gravity. It covers distances $h_1$, $h_2$ and $h_3$ in the first 5 seconds, the next 5 seconds and the next 5 seconds respectively. The relation between $h_1$, $h_2$ and $h_3$ is:
questions.solution:
For a freely falling body, $h = \frac{1}{2}gt^2$.

Distance covered in first 5 seconds ($t = 5$ s):
$h_1 = \frac{1}{2}g(5)^2 = \frac{25}{2}g$

Distance covered in first 10 seconds ($t = 10$ s):
$h_1 + h_2 = \frac{1}{2}g(10)^2 = \frac{100}{2}g$

So, $h_2 = \frac{100}{2}g - \frac{25}{2}g = \frac{75}{2}g = 3h_1$

Distance covered in first 15 seconds ($t = 15$ s):
$h_1 + h_2 + h_3 = \frac{1}{2}g(15)^2 = \frac{225}{2}g$

So, $h_3 = \frac{225}{2}g - \frac{100}{2}g = \frac{125}{2}g = 5h_1$

Therefore, $h_1 = \frac{h_2}{3} = \frac{h_3}{5}$.
questions.explanation: For a freely falling body from rest, distances in successive equal time intervals are in the ratio $1:3:5:7:...$

questions.question_text: A boy standing at the top of a tower of 20 m height drops a stone. Assuming $g = 10$ m/s², the velocity with which it hits the ground is:
questions.solution:
The stone is dropped from rest, so initial velocity $u = 0$.

Height $h = 20$ m, $g = 10$ m/s².

Using the equation $v^2 = u^2 + 2gh$:
$v^2 = 0 + 2 \times 10 \times 20$

$v^2 = 400$

$v = 20$ m/s

So, the stone hits the ground with a velocity of 20 m/s.
questions.explanation: When dropped from rest, use $v = \sqrt{2gh}$.

questions.question_text: A ball is dropped from a high rise platform at $t = 0$ starting from rest. After 6 seconds another ball is thrown downwards from the same platform with a speed $v$. The two balls meet at $t = 18$ s. What is the value of $v$? (Take $g = 10$ m/s²)
questions.solution:
The two balls meet at $t = 18$ s after the first ball is dropped.

For the first ball: $u = 0$, $t = 18$ s, $g = 10$ m/s².

Distance covered: $h = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 18^2$

For the second ball: thrown after 6 s, so time of travel = $18 - 6 = 12$ s.

Let its initial speed be $v$.

Distance covered: $h = v \times 12 + \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 12^2$

Since both cover the same distance:
$\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 18^2 = 12v + \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 12^2$

$5 \times 324 = 12v + 5 \times 144$

$1620 = 12v + 720$

$12v = 900$

$v = 75$ m/s.
questions.explanation: Both balls cover the same distance. For the second ball, time of flight is 12 s (18 - 6).

questions.question_text: A particle starts its motion from rest under the action of a constant force. If the distance covered in first 10 seconds is $S_1$ and that covered in the first 20 seconds is $S_2$, then:
questions.solution:
Starting from rest, $u = 0$.

Distance covered in first 10 seconds:
$S_1 = \frac{1}{2}a(10)^2 = 50a$

Distance covered in first 20 seconds:
$S_2 = \frac{1}{2}a(20)^2 = 200a$

Therefore, $S_2 = 4S_1$.

So, distance covered in first 20 seconds is 4 times the distance covered in first 10 seconds.
questions.explanation: For uniformly accelerated motion from rest, distance is proportional to $t^2$.

questions.question_text: A particle moves in a straight line with a constant acceleration. It changes its velocity from 10 m/s to 20 m/s while passing through a distance 135 m in $t$ second. The value of $t$ is:
questions.solution:
Given: initial velocity $u = 10$ m/s, final velocity $v = 20$ m/s, displacement $s = 135$ m.

Using $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$:
$20^2 = 10^2 + 2 \times a \times 135$

$400 = 100 + 270a$

$300 = 270a$

$a = \frac{10}{9}$ m/s²

Now using $v = u + at$:
$20 = 10 + \frac{10}{9}t$

$10 = \frac{10}{9}t$

$t = 9$ s.
questions.explanation: First find acceleration using $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$, then find time using $v = u + at$.

questions.question_text: The distance travelled by a particle starting from rest and moving with an acceleration $\frac{4}{3}$ m/s² in the third second is:
questions.solution:
Distance travelled in the 3rd second = distance in 3 s - distance in 2 s.

Starting from rest, $u = 0$.

Distance in 3 s: $S_3 = \frac{1}{2}a(3)^2 = \frac{9a}{2}$

Distance in 2 s: $S_2 = \frac{1}{2}a(2)^2 = \frac{4a}{2}$

Distance in 3rd second:
$S_{3rd} = \frac{9a}{2} - \frac{4a}{2} = \frac{5a}{2}$

Given $a = \frac{4}{3}$ m/s²:
$S_{3rd} = \frac{5}{2} \times \frac{4}{3} = \frac{10}{3}$ m.

So, the distance travelled in the 3rd second is $\frac{10}{3}$ m.
questions.explanation: Distance in nth second = $u + \frac{a}{2}(2n - 1)$. For $n = 3$, $u = 0$: $S = \frac{a}{2}(5)$.

questions.question_text: Two bodies A (of mass 1 kg) and B (of mass 3 kg) are dropped from heights of 16 m and 25 m, respectively. The ratio of the time taken by them to reach the ground is:
questions.solution:
Time taken by a body to fall from height $h$ is given by $t = \sqrt{\frac{2h}{g}}$.

For body A: $h_A = 16$ m, so $t_A = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 16}{g}}$

For body B: $h_B = 25$ m, so $t_B = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 25}{g}}$

Ratio: $\frac{t_A}{t_B} = \sqrt{\frac{16}{25}} = \frac{4}{5}$.

The mass of the bodies does not affect the time of fall.
questions.explanation: Time of fall depends only on height and is independent of mass. $t = \sqrt{2h/g}$.

questions.question_text: A ball is thrown vertically upward. It has a speed of 10 m/s when it has reached one half of its maximum height. How high does the ball rise? (Take $g = 10$ m/s²)
questions.solution:
Let the maximum height be $H$.

At half the maximum height, $h = \frac{H}{2}$, speed $v = 10$ m/s.

Using $v^2 = u^2 - 2gh$ (for upward motion):
$10^2 = u^2 - 2 \times 10 \times \frac{H}{2}$

$100 = u^2 - 10H$

At maximum height, $v = 0$: $0 = u^2 - 2 \times 10 \times H$

$u^2 = 20H$

Substituting in the first equation:
$100 = 20H - 10H$

$100 = 10H$

$H = 10$ m.

So, the ball rises to a height of 10 m.
questions.explanation: Use the equations of motion. At maximum height, final velocity is zero. Also, at half the maximum height, the speed is given.

questions.question_text: A man throws balls with the same speed vertically upwards one after the other at an interval of 2 seconds. What should be the speed of the throw so that more than two balls are in the sky at any time? (Given $g = 9.8$ m/s²)
questions.solution:
The time of flight of a ball thrown upward with speed $u$ is $T = \frac{2u}{g}$.

Balls are thrown every 2 seconds.

For more than two balls to be in the air at any time, the time of flight must be greater than 4 seconds.

So, $T > 4$ seconds.

$\frac{2u}{g} > 4$

$u > 2g$

With $g = 9.8$ m/s²:
$u > 2 \times 9.8 = 19.6$ m/s.

So, the speed must be more than 19.6 m/s.
questions.explanation: Time of flight for a vertically thrown ball is $T = 2u/g$. For more than two balls to be in air, $T$ must be greater than the interval between throws times 2.

questions.question_text: If a ball is thrown vertically upwards with speed $u$, the distance covered during the last $t$ seconds of its ascent is:
questions.solution:
During the last $t$ seconds of ascent, the ball is moving upward but slowing down.

Total time of ascent: $T = \frac{u}{g}$.

Distance covered in last $t$ seconds = distance in first $T$ seconds - distance in first $(T - t)$ seconds.

Using $s = ut - \frac{1}{2}gt^2$:

$S_T = uT - \frac{1}{2}gT^2$

$S_{T-t} = u(T-t) - \frac{1}{2}g(T-t)^2$

Distance in last $t$ seconds:
$S = S_T - S_{T-t}$

$S = \frac{1}{2}gt^2$

So, the distance covered during the last $t$ seconds of ascent is $\frac{1}{2}gt^2$, which is independent of the initial speed $u$.
questions.explanation: The last $t$ seconds of ascent is equivalent to the first $t$ seconds of free fall from the maximum height.

questions.question_text: A particle is thrown vertically upward. Its velocity at half of the height is 10 m/s, then the maximum height attained by it (g = 10 m/s²) is:
questions.solution:
Let maximum height be h.

At half the height, velocity v = 10 m/s, and displacement = h/2.

Using v² = u² - 2g(h/2):
10² = u² - gh ...(i)

At maximum height, velocity is zero:
0 = u² - 2gh
u² = 2gh ...(ii)

From (i) and (ii):
100 = 2gh - gh = gh
gh = 100
h = 100/10 = 10 m.

So, the maximum height attained is 10 m.
questions.explanation: Use equations of motion for upward motion. At maximum height, final velocity is zero.

questions.question_text: A car moving with a speed of 40 km/h can be stopped by applying brakes after atleast 2 m. If the same car is moving with a speed of 80 km/h, what is the minimum stopping distance?
questions.solution:
Stopping distance is given by s = u²/(2a), where a is constant retardation.

For first case: u₁ = 40 km/h = 40×5/18 = 100/9 m/s, s₁ = 2 m.

a = u₁²/(2s₁) = (100/9)²/(2×2) = 10000/(81×4) m/s².

For second case: u₂ = 80 km/h = 80×5/18 = 200/9 m/s.

s₂ = u₂²/(2a) = (200/9)² / (2×10000/(81×4))
= (40000/81) / (20000/(81×4))
= 40000/81 × (81×4)/20000 = 8 m.

So, the minimum stopping distance is 8 m.
questions.explanation: Stopping distance is proportional to the square of speed. When speed doubles, stopping distance becomes 4 times.

questions.question_text: If a car at rest accelerates uniformly to a speed of 144 km/h in 20 s, it covers a distance of:
questions.solution:
Initial velocity u = 0.

Final velocity v = 144 km/h = 144×5/18 = 40 m/s.

Time t = 20 s.

Using v = u + at:
40 = 0 + a×20
a = 2 m/s².

Distance covered:
s = ut + ½at² = 0 + ½×2×(20)² = 400 m.

So, the car covers a distance of 400 m.
questions.explanation: First convert speed to m/s, then use equations of motion.

questions.question_text: A body dropped from a height h with initial velocity zero, strikes the ground with a velocity 3 m/s. Another body of same mass dropped from the same height h with an initial velocity of 4 m/s. The final velocity of second mass, with which it strikes the ground is:
questions.solution:
For the first body: u₁ = 0, v₁ = 3 m/s.

Using v₁² = u₁² + 2gh:
3² = 0 + 2gh
2gh = 9
h = 9/(2g).

For the second body: u₂ = 4 m/s.

Using v₂² = u₂² + 2gh:
v₂² = 4² + 9 = 16 + 9 = 25
v₂ = 5 m/s.

So, the final velocity of the second body is 5 m/s.
questions.explanation: First find height using first body's data, then use it to find second body's final velocity.

questions.question_text: The water drop falls at regular intervals from a tap 5 m above the ground. The third drop is leaving the tap at instant the first drop touches the ground. How far above the ground is the second drop at that instant?
questions.solution:
Height of tap = 5 m.

For the first drop: h = ½gt²
5 = ½×10×t²
t² = 1
t = 1 s.

So the first drop takes 1 s to reach the ground.

The third drop leaves when the first drop touches the ground.
So time interval between drops = 1/2 = 0.5 s.

Distance covered by the second drop in 0.5 s:
s = ½×10×(0.5)² = 5×0.25 = 1.25 m.

Height of second drop above ground:
5 - 1.25 = 3.75 m.
questions.explanation: Find time for first drop, then find time interval between drops. Then find distance fallen by second drop.

questions.question_text: A car accelerates from rest at a constant rate α for some time after which it decelerates at a constant rate β and comes to rest. If total time elapsed is t, then maximum velocity acquired by car will be:
questions.solution:
Let maximum velocity be v.

During acceleration: v = αt₁, so t₁ = v/α.

During deceleration: 0 = v - βt₂, so t₂ = v/β.

Total time: t = t₁ + t₂ = v/α + v/β.

t = v(1/α + 1/β) = v(α+β)/(αβ).

Therefore, v = αβt/(α+β).

So, the maximum velocity acquired is $\frac{\alpha \beta t}{\alpha + \beta}$.
questions.explanation: Use v = u + at for both phases. Total time is sum of acceleration and deceleration times.

questions.question_text: The velocity of train increases uniformly from 20 km/h to 60 km/h in 4 hours. The distance travelled by the train during this period is:
questions.solution:
Initial velocity u = 20 km/h, final velocity v = 60 km/h, time t = 4 h.

Using v = u + at:
60 = 20 + a×4
40 = 4a
a = 10 km/h².

Distance travelled:
s = ut + ½at² = 20×4 + ½×10×(4)²
= 80 + ½×10×16 = 80 + 80 = 160 km.

So, the train travels 160 km.
questions.explanation: Find acceleration first, then use s = ut + ½at².

questions.question_text: A body starts from rest, what is the ratio of the distance travelled by the body during the 4th and 3rd second?
questions.solution:
For a body starting from rest, distance in nth second is:
sₙ = ½a(2n - 1).

Distance in 4th second: s₄ = ½a(2×4 - 1) = ½a×7 = 7a/2.

Distance in 3rd second: s₃ = ½a(2×3 - 1) = ½a×5 = 5a/2.

Ratio: s₄/s₃ = (7a/2)/(5a/2) = 7/5.

So, the ratio is 7:5.
questions.explanation: For uniform acceleration from rest, distance in nth second is ½a(2n - 1).

questions.question_text: A body dropped from top of a tower fall through 40 m during the last two seconds of its fall. The height of tower is (g = 10 m/s²):
questions.solution:
Let total time of fall be t seconds.

Distance fallen in last 2 seconds:
40 = ½g[t² - (t-2)²]

40 = 5[t² - (t² - 4t + 4)]
40 = 5(4t - 4)
40 = 20t - 20
60 = 20t
t = 3 s.

Height of tower:
h = ½gt² = ½×10×3² = 5×9 = 45 m.

So, the height of tower is 45 m.
questions.explanation: Distance in last 2 seconds = distance in t seconds - distance in (t-2) seconds.

questions.question_text: What will be the ratio of the distance moved by a freely falling body from rest in 4th and 5th seconds of journey?
questions.solution:
For a freely falling body from rest, distance in nth second is:
sₙ = ½g(2n - 1).

Distance in 4th second: s₄ = ½g(2×4 - 1) = 7g/2.

Distance in 5th second: s₅ = ½g(2×5 - 1) = 9g/2.

Ratio: s₄:s₅ = 7g/2 : 9g/2 = 7:9.

So, the ratio is 7:9.
questions.explanation: For uniform acceleration from rest, distance in nth second is ½g(2n - 1).

questions.question_text: A car is moving along a straight road with a uniform acceleration. It passes through two points P and Q separated by a distance with velocity 30 km/h and 40 km/h respectively. The velocity of the car midway between P and Q is:
questions.solution:
Let distance PQ = s, and midway velocity be v.

Using v² = u² + 2as from P to Q:
40² - 30² = 2as
1600 - 900 = 2as
700 = 2as
a = 350/s ...(i)

Now from P to midway (distance = s/2):
v² - 30² = 2a(s/2) = as

v² = 900 + as = 900 + 350 = 1250
v = √1250 = 25√2 km/h.

So, the velocity at midway is 25√2 km/h.
questions.explanation: Use v² = u² + 2as for both segments. Find acceleration first, then use it for the midway point.

questions.question_text: Preeti reached the metro station and found that the escalator was not working. She walked up the stationary escalator in time $t_1$. On other days, if she remains stationary on the moving escalator, then the escalator takes her up in time $t_2$. The time taken by her to walk up on the moving escalator will be:
questions.solution:
Let $d$ be the distance travelled by Preeti.

Her speed on stationary escalator: $v_1 = \frac{d}{t_1}$

Speed of moving escalator: $v_2 = \frac{d}{t_2}$

When she walks on the moving escalator, both move in same direction, so net speed is sum:
$v = v_1 + v_2 = \frac{d}{t_1} + \frac{d}{t_2} = d\left(\frac{t_1 + t_2}{t_1 t_2}\right)$

Time taken: $t = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{d}{d\left(\frac{t_1 + t_2}{t_1 t_2}\right)} = \frac{t_1 t_2}{t_1 + t_2}$

$\boxed{t = \frac{t_1 t_2}{t_1 + t_2}}$
questions.explanation: When moving in same direction, speeds add up. Time = distance/speed.

questions.question_text: A bus is moving with a speed of $10$ m/s on a straight road. A scooterist wishes to overtake the bus in $100$ s. If the bus is at a distance of $1$ km from the scooterist, with what speed should the scooterist chase the bus?
questions.solution:
Distance between scooter and bus: $s = 1000$ m.

Bus speed: $v_b = 10$ m/s.

Let scooter speed be $v_s$.

Since both move in same direction, relative speed: $v_{\text{rel}} = v_s - v_b = v_s - 10$

Time to overtake: $t = 100$ s.

$\frac{1000}{v_s - 10} = 100$

$1000 = 100(v_s - 10)$

$v_s - 10 = 10$

$\boxed{v_s = 20 \text{ m/s}}$
questions.explanation: Relative speed = difference of speeds when moving in same direction. Time = distance/relative speed.

questions.question_text: A train of $150$ metre length is going towards north direction at a speed of $10$ m/s. A parrot flies at the speed of $5$ m/s towards south direction parallel to the railways track. The time taken by the parrot to cross the train is:
questions.solution:
Train speed: $v_T = 10$ m/s (north).

Parrot speed: $v_P = 5$ m/s (south).

Since they move in opposite directions, relative speed is sum:
$v_{\text{rel}} = v_T + v_P = 10 + 5 = 15$ m/s.

Length of train: $L = 150$ m.

Time taken by parrot to cross the train:
$t = \frac{L}{v_{\text{rel}}} = \frac{150}{15} = 10$ s.

$\boxed{t = 10 \text{ s}}$
questions.explanation: When moving in opposite directions, relative speed = sum of speeds.

questions.question_text: A car starts from rest and accelerates at $5$ m/s$^2$. At $t = 4$ s, a ball is dropped out of a window by a person sitting in the car. What is the velocity and acceleration of the ball at $t = 6$ s? (Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
questions.solution:
Car acceleration: $a = 5$ m/s$^2$, initial velocity $u = 0$.

At $t = 4$ s, car velocity: $v = u + at = 0 + 5 \times 4 = 20$ m/s.

At $t = 6$ s, the ball has been in air for $2$ s.

Horizontal velocity of ball remains constant: $v_x = 20$ m/s.

Vertical velocity: $v_y = g \times t = 10 \times 2 = 20$ m/s.

Net velocity: $v = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2} = \sqrt{20^2 + 20^2} = 20\sqrt{2}$ m/s.

Acceleration: Only gravity acts on the ball, so $a = 10$ m/s$^2$ downward.

Horizontal acceleration: $a_x = 0$.

$\boxed{v = 20\sqrt{2} \text{ m/s}, \; a = 10 \text{ m/s}^2}$
questions.explanation: After dropping, horizontal velocity remains constant, vertical velocity increases due to gravity.

questions.question_text: A small block slides down on a smooth inclined plane, starting from rest at time $t = 0$. Let $s_n$ be the distance travelled by the block in the interval $t = n - 1$ to $t = n$. Then, the ratio $\frac{s_n}{s_{n+1}}$ is:
questions.solution:
For uniformly accelerated motion starting from rest, distance in $n$th second is:
$s_n = \frac{a}{2}(2n - 1)$

Distance in $(n+1)$th second:
$s_{n+1} = \frac{a}{2}[2(n+1) - 1] = \frac{a}{2}(2n + 1)$

Ratio:
$\frac{s_n}{s_{n+1}} = \frac{\frac{a}{2}(2n - 1)}{\frac{a}{2}(2n + 1)}$

$\boxed{\frac{s_n}{s_{n+1}} = \frac{2n - 1}{2n + 1}}$
questions.explanation: Distance in nth second formula: $s_n = u + \frac{a}{2}(2n - 1)$. Since $u = 0$, $s_n = \frac{a}{2}(2n - 1)$.

questions.question_text: The displacement-time graphs of two moving particles make angles of $30^\circ$ and $45^\circ$ with the x-axis as shown in the figure. The ratio of their respective velocities is:
questions.solution:
Slope of displacement-time graph gives velocity: $v = \tan \theta$.

For first particle: $\theta_1 = 30^\circ$, $v_1 = \tan 30^\circ = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$.

For second particle: $\theta_2 = 45^\circ$, $v_2 = \tan 45^\circ = 1$.

Ratio: $\frac{v_1}{v_2} = \frac{1/\sqrt{3}}{1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$

$\boxed{v_1 : v_2 = 1 : \sqrt{3}}$
questions.explanation: Velocity is the slope of displacement-time graph. Slope = tan θ.

questions.question_text: The ratio of the distances travelled by a freely falling body in the 1st, 2nd, 3rd and 4th second is:
questions.solution:
For a freely falling body starting from rest, distance in $n$th second is proportional to $(2n - 1)$.

1st second: $2(1) - 1 = 1$

2nd second: $2(2) - 1 = 3$

3rd second: $2(3) - 1 = 5$

4th second: $2(4) - 1 = 7$

$\boxed{1 : 3 : 5 : 7}$
questions.explanation: Distances in successive seconds for a freely falling body are in the ratio 1:3:5:7...

questions.question_text: A football player is moving southward and suddenly turns eastward with the same speed to avoid an opponent. The force that acts on the player while turning is:
questions.solution:
Initial velocity: $\vec{u} = -v\hat{j}$ (south direction).

Final velocity: $\vec{v} = v\hat{i}$ (east direction).

Change in velocity: $\Delta\vec{v} = \vec{v} - \vec{u} = v\hat{i} - (-v\hat{j}) = v\hat{i} + v\hat{j}$

This change is along north-east direction.

Force acts along the direction of change in velocity (Newton's second law).

$\boxed{\text{Force acts along north-east direction}}$
questions.explanation: Force is along the direction of change in velocity. When direction changes, force acts along the resultant of initial and final velocities.

questions.question_text: A vehicle travels half the distance with speed $v$ and the remaining distance with speed $2v$. Its average speed is:
questions.solution:
Let total distance be $2x$.

Time for first half: $t_1 = \frac{x}{v}$

Time for second half: $t_2 = \frac{x}{2v}$

Total time: $t = t_1 + t_2 = \frac{x}{v} + \frac{x}{2v} = \frac{2x + x}{2v} = \frac{3x}{2v}$

Average speed: $v_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\text{Total distance}}{\text{Total time}} = \frac{2x}{3x/2v} = \frac{4v}{3}$

$\boxed{v_{\text{avg}} = \frac{4v}{3}}$
questions.explanation: Average speed = total distance / total time. When distances are equal, use harmonic mean.

questions.question_text: The velocity (v) - time (t) plot of the motion of a body is shown below. The acceleration (a) - time (t) graph that best suits this motion is:
questions.solution:
Acceleration is the slope of velocity-time graph.

When the graph has positive slope, acceleration is positive (above zero).

When the graph has zero slope, acceleration is zero.

When the graph has negative slope, acceleration is negative (below zero).

The acceleration-time graph must show these regions clearly.

$\boxed{\text{Option (a) correctly represents the acceleration-time graph}}$
questions.explanation: Acceleration is the slope of v-t graph. Positive slope = positive acceleration, negative slope = negative acceleration, zero slope = zero acceleration.

questions.question_text: In some appropriate units, time $t$ and position $x$ relation of moving particle is given by $t = x^2 + x$. The acceleration of the particle is:
questions.solution:
Given: $t = x^2 + x$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $t$:
$1 = (2x + 1)\frac{dx}{dt}$

So, $v = \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{1}{2x + 1}$

Acceleration: $a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{dv}{dx} \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}$

$\frac{dv}{dx} = \frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{1}{2x + 1}\right) = -\frac{2}{(2x + 1)^2}$

$a = -\frac{2}{(2x + 1)^2} \times \frac{1}{2x + 1} = -\frac{2}{(2x + 1)^3}$

$\boxed{a = -\frac{2}{(2x + 1)^3}}$
questions.explanation: First find velocity by differentiating t with respect to x. Then find acceleration using chain rule.

questions.question_text: Two cities X and Y are connected by a regular bus service with a bus leaving in either direction every $T$ min. A girl is driving scooty with a speed of $60$ km/h in the direction X to Y notices that a bus goes past her every $30$ minutes in the direction of her motion, and every $10$ minutes in the opposite direction. Choose the correct option for the period $T$ of the bus service and the speed (assumed constant) of the buses.
questions.solution:
Girl's speed: $v_g = 60$ km/h.

Let bus speed be $v_b$.

When moving in same direction: relative speed $= v_b - 60$, time $= 30$ min $= 0.5$ h.
Distance between buses: $d = (v_b - 60) \times 0.5$ ...(i)

When moving in opposite direction: relative speed $= v_b + 60$, time $= 10$ min $= \frac{1}{6}$ h.
Distance between buses: $d = (v_b + 60) \times \frac{1}{6}$ ...(ii)

Equating (i) and (ii):
$\frac{v_b - 60}{2} = \frac{v_b + 60}{6}$

$3(v_b - 60) = v_b + 60$

$3v_b - 180 = v_b + 60$

$2v_b = 240$

$v_b = 120$ km/h.

Distance between buses: $d = \frac{120 - 60}{2} = 30$ km.

Time interval: $T = \frac{d}{v_b} = \frac{30}{120} = 0.25$ h $= 15$ minutes.

$\boxed{T = 15 \text{ min}, \; v_b = 120 \text{ km/h}}$
questions.explanation: Use relative velocity concept. Distance between buses is constant. Equate the two expressions for distance.

questions.question_text: The unit of thermal conductivity is
questions.solution:
The formula for thermal conductivity is:
$K = \dfrac{Q \times x}{A \times (T_1 - T_2) \times t}$

Substituting units:
$K = \dfrac{\text{J} \times \text{m}}{\text{m}^2 \times \text{K} \times \text{s}}$
$K = \text{J m}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1} \text{s}^{-1}$

Since $1 \text{ W} = 1 \text{ J/s}$,
$K = \text{W m}^{-1} \text{K}^{-1}$

$\boxed{\text{Option (a) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Thermal conductivity measures a material's ability to conduct heat. Its SI unit is W m⁻¹ K⁻¹.

questions.question_text: The damping force on an oscillator is directly proportional to the velocity. The units of the constant of proportionality are
questions.solution:
Damping force is given by:
$F \propto v$ or $F = kv$
where $k$ is the constant of proportionality.

Rearranging: $k = \dfrac{F}{v}$

Unit of force: $F = \text{kg m s}^{-2}$
Unit of velocity: $v = \text{m s}^{-1}$

Therefore:
$k = \dfrac{\text{kg m s}^{-2}}{\text{m s}^{-1}}$
$k = \text{kg s}^{-1}$

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: The damping constant (or damping coefficient) relates the damping force to velocity. Its SI unit is kg s⁻¹.

questions.question_text: The unit of permittivity of free space, $\varepsilon_0$, is
questions.solution:
From Coulomb's law:
$F = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$

Rearranging to find $\varepsilon_0$:
$\varepsilon_0 = \dfrac{q_1 q_2}{4\pi F r^2}$

Substituting units (ignoring $4\pi$ which is dimensionless):
$\varepsilon_0 = \dfrac{\text{C} \times \text{C}}{\text{N} \times \text{m}^2}$
$\varepsilon_0 = \text{C}^2 \, \text{N}^{-1} \, \text{m}^{-2}$

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Permittivity of free space (ε₀) is a fundamental physical constant. Its SI unit is C² N⁻¹ m⁻² or F m⁻¹.

questions.question_text: A screw gauge has least count of 0.01 mm and there are 50 divisions in its circular scale. The pitch of the screw gauge is
questions.solution:
The relationship between Least Count (LC), Pitch, and number of circular scale divisions is:
$\text{LC} = \dfrac{\text{Pitch}}{\text{Number of divisions}}$

Rearranging:
$\text{Pitch} = \text{LC} \times \text{Number of divisions}$

Substituting the values:
$\text{Pitch} = 0.01 \text{ mm} \times 50$
$\text{Pitch} = 0.5 \text{ mm}$

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Pitch is the distance moved by the screw in one complete rotation. It can be calculated as LC × Number of circular scale divisions.

questions.question_text: In an experiment, the percentage of error occurred in the measurement of physical quantities $A, B, C$ and $D$ are 1%, 2%, 3% and 4% respectively. Then the maximum percentage of error in the measurement $X$, where $X = \dfrac{A^2 B^{1/2}}{C^{1/3} D^3}$, will be
questions.solution:
Given: $X = A^2 B^{1/2} C^{-1/3} D^{-3}$

For maximum percentage error, all errors add up:
$\dfrac{\Delta X}{X} \times 100 = 2\left(\dfrac{\Delta A}{A}\right) + \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\Delta B}{B}\right) + \dfrac{1}{3}\left(\dfrac{\Delta C}{C}\right) + 3\left(\dfrac{\Delta D}{D}\right)$

Substituting the given percentages:
$= 2(1\%) + \dfrac{1}{2}(2\%) + \dfrac{1}{3}(3\%) + 3(4\%)$
$= 2\% + 1\% + 1\% + 12\%$
$= 16\%$

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: For maximum percentage error in a physical quantity, the errors in individual measurements are added with their respective powers.

questions.question_text: The main scale of a vernier callipers has $n$ divisions/cm. $n$ divisions of the vernier scale coincide with $(n-1)$ divisions of main scale. The least count of the vernier callipers is
questions.solution:
Given: $n$ divisions of vernier scale = $(n-1)$ divisions of main scale
$n \text{ VSD} = (n-1) \text{ MSD}$

Therefore:
$1 \text{ VSD} = \dfrac{(n-1)}{n} \text{ MSD}$

Least Count = 1 MSD $-$ 1 VSD
$\text{LC} = 1 \text{ MSD} - \dfrac{(n-1)}{n} \text{ MSD}$
$\text{LC} = \dfrac{1}{n} \text{ MSD}$

Since 1 MSD = $\dfrac{1}{n}$ cm,
$\text{LC} = \dfrac{1}{n} \times \dfrac{1}{n} = \dfrac{1}{n^2}$ cm

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Least count of a vernier callipers is the difference between one main scale division and one vernier scale division.

questions.question_text: A student measured the diameter of a small steel ball using a screw gauge of least count 0.001 cm. The main scale reading is 5 mm and zero of circular scale division coincides with 25 divisions above the reference level. If screw gauge has a zero error of $-0.004$ cm, the correct diameter of the ball is
questions.solution:
Given:
MSR = 5 mm = 0.5 cm
CSR = 25 divisions
LC = 0.001 cm
Zero error = $-0.004$ cm

Observed diameter = MSR + CSR $\times$ LC
$= 0.5 \text{ cm} + 25 \times 0.001 \text{ cm}$
$= 0.5 \text{ cm} + 0.025 \text{ cm}$
$= 0.525 \text{ cm}$

Correct diameter = Observed $-$ Zero error
$= 0.525 - (-0.004)$
$= 0.529 \text{ cm}$

$\boxed{\text{Option (d) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: When using a screw gauge with zero error, the correct reading is obtained by subtracting the zero error from the observed reading.

questions.question_text: In an experiment, four quantities $a, b, c$ and $d$ are measured with percentage error 1%, 2%, 3% and 4% respectively. Quantity $P$ is calculated as $P = \dfrac{a^3 b^2}{c d}$. The percentage error in $P$ is
questions.solution:
Given: $P = a^3 b^2 c^{-1} d^{-1}$

For maximum percentage error:
$\dfrac{\Delta P}{P} \times 100 = 3\left(\dfrac{\Delta a}{a}\right) + 2\left(\dfrac{\Delta b}{b}\right) + \left(\dfrac{\Delta c}{c}\right) + \left(\dfrac{\Delta d}{d}\right)$

Substituting the given percentages:
$= 3(1\%) + 2(2\%) + 3\% + 4\%$
$= 3\% + 4\% + 3\% + 4\%$
$= 14\%$

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: The percentage error in a quantity is the sum of the percentage errors of the individual quantities multiplied by their respective powers.

questions.question_text: A student measures the distance traversed in free fall of a body, initially at rest, in a given time. He uses this data to estimate $g$, the acceleration due to gravity. If the maximum percentage errors in measurement of the distance and the time are $e_1$ and $e_2$ respectively, the percentage error in the estimation of $g$ is
questions.solution:
From the equation of motion for a body initially at rest:
$h = \dfrac{1}{2} g t^2$

Rearranging to find $g$:
$g = \dfrac{2h}{t^2}$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:
$\ln g = \ln 2 + \ln h - 2 \ln t$

Differentiating:
$\dfrac{\Delta g}{g} = \dfrac{\Delta h}{h} + 2\left(\dfrac{\Delta t}{t}\right)$

For maximum permissible error, all errors add up:
$\left(\dfrac{\Delta g}{g}\right) \times 100 = \left(\dfrac{\Delta h}{h}\right) \times 100 + 2\left(\dfrac{\Delta t}{t}\right) \times 100$

Given: $\left(\dfrac{\Delta h}{h}\right) \times 100 = e_1$ and $\left(\dfrac{\Delta t}{t}\right) \times 100 = e_2$

Therefore:
$\left(\dfrac{\Delta g}{g}\right) \times 100 = e_1 + 2e_2$

$\boxed{\text{Option (b) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: The percentage error in g is calculated using the propagation of errors formula. Since g ∝ h/t², the error is e₁ + 2e₂.

questions.question_text: If the error in the measurement of radius of a sphere is 2%, then the error in the determination of volume of the sphere will be
questions.solution:
Volume of a sphere is given by:
$V = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R^3$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:
$\ln V = \ln\left(\dfrac{4}{3}\pi\right) + 3 \ln R$

Differentiating:
$\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} = 3\left(\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}\right)$

Therefore, percentage error in volume:
$\left(\dfrac{\Delta V}{V}\right) \times 100 = 3 \times \left(\dfrac{\Delta R}{R}\right) \times 100$
$= 3 \times 2\%$
$= 6\%$

$\boxed{\text{Option (d) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Volume of a sphere is proportional to R³. So, the percentage error in volume is 3 times the percentage error in radius.

questions.question_text: The density of a cube is measured by measuring its mass and length of its sides. If the maximum error in the measurement of mass and lengths are 3% and 2% respectively, the maximum error in the measurement of density would be
questions.solution:
Density, $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{m}{l^3}$

Maximum error in density:
$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \times 100 = \dfrac{\Delta m}{m} \times 100 + 3\left(\dfrac{\Delta l}{l} \times 100\right)$

Given: $\dfrac{\Delta m}{m} \times 100 = 3\%$ and $\dfrac{\Delta l}{l} \times 100 = 2\%$

$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \times 100 = 3\% + 3(2\%) = 3\% + 6\% = 9\%$

$\boxed{\text{Option (d) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Density depends on mass and volume (l³). So error in density = error in mass + 3 × error in length.

questions.question_text: Percentage errors in the measurement of mass and speed are 2% and 3% respectively. The error in the estimate of kinetic energy obtained by measuring mass and speed will be
questions.solution:
Kinetic energy, $K = \dfrac{1}{2} mv^2$

Maximum error in kinetic energy:
$\dfrac{\Delta K}{K} \times 100 = \dfrac{\Delta m}{m} \times 100 + 2\left(\dfrac{\Delta v}{v} \times 100\right)$

Given: $\dfrac{\Delta m}{m} \times 100 = 2\%$ and $\dfrac{\Delta v}{v} \times 100 = 3\%$

$\dfrac{\Delta K}{K} \times 100 = 2\% + 2(3\%) = 2\% + 6\% = 8\%$

$\boxed{\text{Option (a) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy depends on mass (power 1) and speed (power 2). So error in K.E. = error in mass + 2 × error in speed.

questions.question_text: A certain body weighs 22.42 g and has a measured volume of 4.7 cc. The possible error in the measurement of mass and volume are 0.01 g and 0.1 cc. Then maximum error in the density will be
questions.solution:
Density, $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$

Maximum error in density:
$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \times 100 = \left(\dfrac{\Delta m}{m} + \dfrac{\Delta V}{V}\right) \times 100$

Given: $\Delta m = 0.01$ g, $m = 22.42$ g, $\Delta V = 0.1$ cc, $V = 4.7$ cc

$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \times 100 = \left(\dfrac{0.01}{22.42} + \dfrac{0.1}{4.7}\right) \times 100$

$= (0.000446 + 0.021277) \times 100$

$= 0.021723 \times 100 \approx 2\%$

$\boxed{\text{Option (b) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Error in density is the sum of relative errors in mass and volume, expressed as percentage.

questions.question_text: Taking into account of the significant figures, what is the value of $9.99\text{ m} - 0.0099\text{ m}$?
questions.solution:
$9.99 - 0.0099 = 9.9801$ m

Rule for addition/subtraction: The result should have the same number of decimal places as the quantity with the least number of decimal places.

9.99 has 2 decimal places.
0.0099 has 4 decimal places.

Therefore, the answer should be rounded to 2 decimal places.

$9.9801 \rightarrow 9.98$ m

$\boxed{\text{Option (b) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: In addition/subtraction, the result should be rounded to the least number of decimal places among the given quantities.

questions.question_text: Dimensions of stress are
questions.solution:
Stress = $\dfrac{\text{Force}}{\text{Area}}$

Dimension of force = $[MLT^{-2}]$
Dimension of area = $[L^2]$

$[\text{Stress}] = \dfrac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[L^2]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (d) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Stress is defined as force per unit area. Its dimensions are ML⁻¹T⁻², which is same as pressure.

questions.question_text: The pair of quantities having same dimensions is
questions.solution:
Work = Force $\times$ distance = $[MLT^{-2}][L] = [ML^2T^{-2}]$

Torque = Force $\times$ perpendicular distance = $[MLT^{-2}][L] = [ML^2T^{-2}]$

Hence, Work and Torque have the same dimensions.

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Both work and torque have dimensions of ML²T⁻². Work is scalar, torque is vector but both have same dimensional formula.

questions.question_text: The dimensions of $(\mu_0 \varepsilon_0)^{-1/2}$ are
questions.solution:
The speed of light in vacuum is given by:
$c = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}} = (\mu_0 \varepsilon_0)^{-1/2}$

Therefore, $(\mu_0 \varepsilon_0)^{-1/2} = [c] = [LT^{-1}]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: The expression (μ₀ε₀)⁻¹/² represents the speed of light in vacuum, whose dimension is LT⁻¹.

questions.question_text: The dimension of $\dfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^2$, where $\varepsilon_0$ is permittivity of free space and $E$ is electric field, is
questions.solution:
$\dfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^2$ represents energy density of electric field.

Energy density = $\dfrac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Volume}}$

Dimension of energy = $[ML^2T^{-2}]$
Dimension of volume = $[L^3]$

$[\text{Energy density}] = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-2}]}{[L^3]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (b) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: The expression ½ε₀E² is the energy density of electric field. Its dimension is energy per unit volume.

questions.question_text: If the dimensions of a physical quantity are given by $M^a L^b T^c$, then the physical quantity will be
questions.solution:
Pressure = $\dfrac{\text{Force}}{\text{Area}} = \dfrac{\text{Mass} \times \text{Acceleration}}{\text{Area}}$

$[\text{Pressure}] = \dfrac{[M][LT^{-2}]}{[L^2]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$

Therefore, $a = 1, b = -1, c = -2$

$\boxed{\text{Option (d) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Pressure has dimensions ML⁻¹T⁻², which corresponds to M¹L⁻¹T⁻².

questions.question_text:
Which two of the following five physical parameters have the same dimensions?
1. energy density
2. refractive index
3. dielectric constant
4. Young's modulus
5. magnetic field
questions.solution:
Energy density = $\dfrac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Volume}} = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-2}]}{[L^3]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$

Young's modulus = $\dfrac{\text{Stress}}{\text{Strain}} = \dfrac{[ML^{-1}T^{-2}]}{\text{dimensionless}} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$

Refractive index = dimensionless
Dielectric constant = dimensionless
Magnetic field = $[MT^{-2}A^{-1}]$

Therefore, energy density and Young's modulus have the same dimensions.

$\boxed{\text{Option (a) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Energy density and Young's modulus both have dimensions ML⁻¹T⁻². Refractive index and dielectric constant are dimensionless.

questions.question_text: Dimensions of resistance in an electrical circuit, in terms of dimension of mass M, of length L, of time T and of current I, would be
questions.solution:
From Ohm's law: $V = IR \Rightarrow R = \dfrac{V}{I}$

Voltage $V = \dfrac{\text{Work}}{\text{Charge}} = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-2}]}{[IT]} = [ML^2T^{-3}I^{-1}]$

Therefore, $R = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-3}I^{-1}]}{[I]} = [ML^2T^{-3}I^{-2}]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Resistance in SI units is ohm (Ω). Its dimensional formula is ML²T⁻³I⁻².

questions.question_text: The ratio of the dimensions of Planck's constant and that of moment of inertia is the dimensions of
questions.solution:
Planck's constant, $h = \dfrac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Frequency}} = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-2}]}{[T^{-1}]} = [ML^2T^{-1}]$

Moment of inertia, $I = [ML^2]$

$\dfrac{h}{I} = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-1}]}{[ML^2]} = [T^{-1}]$ = frequency

$\boxed{\text{Option (b) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Planck's constant has dimensions of angular momentum (ML²T⁻¹). Dividing by moment of inertia (ML²) gives T⁻¹, which is frequency.

questions.question_text: The dimensions of universal gravitational constant are
questions.solution:
From Newton's law of gravitation:
$F = \dfrac{G m_1 m_2}{r^2}$

Rearranging: $G = \dfrac{F r^2}{m_1 m_2}$

$[G] = \dfrac{[MLT^{-2}][L^2]}{[M][M]} = [M^{-1}L^3T^{-2}]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (a) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Gravitational constant G has dimensions M⁻¹L³T⁻². Its SI unit is N m² kg⁻².

questions.question_text: The dimensions of Planck's constant equals to that of
questions.solution:
Planck's constant, $h = \dfrac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Frequency}} = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-2}]}{[T^{-1}]} = [ML^2T^{-1}]$

Angular momentum = Moment of inertia $\times$ Angular velocity
$= [ML^2][T^{-1}] = [ML^2T^{-1}]$

Hence, Planck's constant has the same dimensions as angular momentum.

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Planck's constant (h) and angular momentum both have dimensions ML²T⁻¹.

questions.question_text: Which pair do not have equal dimensions?
questions.solution:
Force = $[MLT^{-2}]$
Impulse = Force $\times$ Time = $[MLT^{-2}][T] = [MLT^{-1}]$

Force and impulse have different dimensions.

$\boxed{\text{Option (b) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Force has dimensions MLT⁻² while impulse has MLT⁻¹. All other pairs have same dimensions.

questions.question_text: The dimensions of impulse are equal to that of
questions.solution:
Impulse = Force $\times$ Time = $[MLT^{-2}][T] = [MLT^{-1}]$

Linear momentum = Mass $\times$ Velocity = $[M][LT^{-1}] = [MLT^{-1}]$

Hence, impulse has the same dimensions as linear momentum.

$\boxed{\text{Option (b) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Impulse and linear momentum both have dimensions MLT⁻¹. Impulse is equal to change in momentum.

questions.question_text: Which of the following dimensions will be the same as that of time?
questions.solution:
Time constant of an LR circuit, $\tau = \dfrac{L}{R}$ has dimensions of time.

$\dfrac{L}{R} = [T]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (a) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: L/R is the time constant in an LR circuit, which has dimensions of time (T).

questions.question_text: The dimensions of $RC$ is
questions.solution:
RC is the time constant of an RC circuit.

$RC = [T]$ (time)

Units of RC = ohm $\times$ farad = second

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: RC is the time constant in a capacitor-resistor circuit. Its dimension is time (T).

questions.question_text: Which of the following has the dimensions of pressure?
questions.solution:
Pressure = $\dfrac{\text{Force}}{\text{Area}} = \dfrac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[L^2]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (b) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Pressure is force per unit area. Its dimensions are ML⁻¹T⁻².

questions.question_text: Of the following quantities, which one has dimensions different from the remaining three?
questions.option_c: Product of voltage and charge per unit volume
questions.solution:
Energy per unit volume = $\dfrac{[ML^2T^{-2}]}{[L^3]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$

Force per unit area = $\dfrac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[L^2]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$

Voltage $\times$ charge per unit volume = $\dfrac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Volume}} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$

Angular momentum = $[ML^2T^{-1}]$

Angular momentum has different dimensions.

$\boxed{\text{Option (d) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Energy density, pressure, and voltage×charge/volume all have dimensions ML⁻¹T⁻². Angular momentum has ML²T⁻¹.

questions.question_text: The dimensional formula of magnetic flux is
questions.solution:
Magnetic flux, $\phi = BA$

Also, $B = \dfrac{F}{Il}$ (Force per unit current per unit length)

$[\phi] = \left[\dfrac{F}{Il}\right] \times [A]$

$[\phi] = \dfrac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[A][L]} \times [L^2]$

$[\phi] = \dfrac{[MLT^{-2}][L^2]}{[A][L]} = [ML^2T^{-2}A^{-1}]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Magnetic flux has dimensions ML²T⁻²A⁻¹. Its SI unit is Weber (Wb).

questions.question_text: The dimensional formula of permeability of free space $\mu_0$ is
questions.solution:
Force between two parallel current-carrying conductors:
$F = \dfrac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2 l}{2\pi r}$

Rearranging for $\mu_0$:
$\mu_0 = \dfrac{F \times 2\pi r}{I_1 I_2 l}$

$[\mu_0] = \dfrac{[MLT^{-2}][L]}{[A][A][L]} = [MLT^{-2}A^{-2}]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (a) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Permeability of free space (μ₀) has dimensions MLT⁻²A⁻². Its SI unit is H m⁻¹ or N A⁻².

questions.question_text: According to Newton, the viscous force acting between liquid layers of area $A$ and velocity gradient $\Delta v / \Delta z$ is given by $F = -\eta A \dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta z}$, where $\eta$ is constant called coefficient of viscosity. The dimensional formula of $\eta$ is
questions.solution:
Given: $F = -\eta A \dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta z}$

Rearranging for $\eta$:
$\eta = \dfrac{F}{A \cdot (\Delta v / \Delta z)}$

Dimensions:
$[F] = [MLT^{-2}]$
$[A] = [L^2]$
$\left[\dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta z}\right] = \dfrac{[LT^{-1}]}{[L]} = [T^{-1}]$

$[\eta] = \dfrac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[L^2][T^{-1}]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-1}]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (d) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Coefficient of viscosity (η) has dimensions ML⁻¹T⁻¹. Its SI unit is Pa·s or N·s/m².

questions.question_text: Dimensional formula of self inductance is
questions.solution:
Induced emf, $|\varepsilon| = L \dfrac{dI}{dt}$

Rearranging for $L$:
$L = \dfrac{|\varepsilon|}{dI/dt}$

Dimensions of emf ($\varepsilon$) = Voltage = $[ML^2T^{-3}A^{-1}]$
Dimensions of $\dfrac{dI}{dt}$ = $[AT^{-1}]$

$[L] = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-3}A^{-1}]}{[AT^{-1}]} = [ML^2T^{-2}A^{-2}]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (b) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Self inductance (L) has dimensions ML²T⁻²A⁻². Its SI unit is Henry (H).

questions.question_text: The dimensional formula of torque is
questions.solution:
Torque, $\tau = \text{Force} \times \text{Perpendicular distance}$

$[\tau] = [MLT^{-2}] \times [L]$

$[\tau] = [ML^2T^{-2}]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (a) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Torque has dimensions ML²T⁻², which is the same as energy and work.

questions.question_text: If $C$ and $R$ denote capacitance and resistance, the dimensional formula of $CR$ is
questions.option_d: not expressible in terms of MLT
questions.solution:
Capacitance, $C = \dfrac{\text{Charge}}{\text{Potential difference}}$

$[C] = \dfrac{[AT]}{[ML^2T^{-3}A^{-1}]} = [M^{-1}L^{-2}T^4A^2]$

Resistance, $R = \dfrac{\text{Potential difference}}{\text{Current}}$

$[R] = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-3}A^{-1}]}{[A]} = [ML^2T^{-3}A^{-2}]$

$[CR] = [M^{-1}L^{-2}T^4A^2] \times [ML^2T^{-3}A^{-2}]$

$[CR] = [T]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (a) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: CR is the time constant of an RC circuit. Its dimension is time (T).

questions.question_text: The dimensional formula of angular momentum is
questions.solution:
Angular momentum, $L = I\omega$

where $I$ = Moment of inertia and $\omega$ = Angular velocity

$[I] = [ML^2]$
$[\omega] = [T^{-1}]$

$[L] = [ML^2] \times [T^{-1}] = [ML^2T^{-1}]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (d) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Angular momentum has dimensions ML²T⁻¹, which is the same as Planck's constant.

questions.question_text: A physical quantity of the dimensions of length that can be formed out of $c$, $G$ and $\dfrac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}$ is [$c$ is velocity of light, $G$ is the universal constant of gravitation and $e$ is charge]
questions.solution:
Let $l \propto \left(\dfrac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\right)^p G^q c^r$

Dimensions:
$\left[\dfrac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\right] = [F \times r^2] = [ML^3T^{-2}]$
$[G] = [M^{-1}L^3T^{-2}]$
$[c] = [LT^{-1}]$

$[L] = [ML^3T^{-2}]^p [M^{-1}L^3T^{-2}]^q [LT^{-1}]^r$

Comparing powers of M, L, T:

M: $p - q = 0 \Rightarrow p = q$

L: $3p + 3q + r = 1$

T: $-2p - 2q - r = 0$

Solving: $p = q = \dfrac{1}{2}, r = -2$

$l \propto \dfrac{1}{c^2}\left[G\dfrac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\right]^{1/2}$

$\boxed{\text{Option (b) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: By dimensional analysis, combining these constants gives a quantity with dimensions of length. The correct combination is (1/c²)√(G e²/4πε₀).

questions.question_text: Planck's constant ($h$), speed of light in vacuum ($c$) and Newton's gravitational constant ($G$) are three fundamental constants. Which of the following combinations of these has the dimension of length?
questions.solution:
Let $l \propto h^p c^q G^r$

Dimensions:
$[h] = [ML^2T^{-1}]$
$[c] = [LT^{-1}]$
$[G] = [M^{-1}L^3T^{-2}]$
$[l] = [L]$

$[L] = [ML^2T^{-1}]^p [LT^{-1}]^q [M^{-1}L^3T^{-2}]^r$

Comparing powers of M, L, T:

M: $p - r = 0 \Rightarrow p = r$

L: $2p + q + 3r = 1$

T: $-p - q - 2r = 0$

Solving: $p = r = \dfrac{1}{2}, q = -\dfrac{3}{2}$

$l \propto \dfrac{\sqrt{hG}}{c^{3/2}}$

$\boxed{\text{Option (a) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: This is the Planck length formula. By dimensional analysis, l = √(hG/c³).

questions.question_text: If dimensions of critical velocity $v_c$ of a liquid flowing through a tube are expressed as $[\eta^x \rho^y r^z]$ where $\eta$, $\rho$ and $r$ are the coefficient of viscosity of liquid, density of liquid and radius of the tube respectively, then the values of $x, y$ and $z$ are given by
questions.solution:
Given: $[v_c] = [\eta^x \rho^y r^z]$

Dimensions:
$[v_c] = [LT^{-1}]$
$[\eta] = [ML^{-1}T^{-1}]$
$[\rho] = [ML^{-3}]$
$[r] = [L]$

$[LT^{-1}] = [ML^{-1}T^{-1}]^x [ML^{-3}]^y [L]^z$

$[LT^{-1}] = [M^{x+y} L^{-x-3y+z} T^{-x}]$

Comparing powers:

M: $x + y = 0$

T: $-x = -1 \Rightarrow x = 1$

Therefore, $y = -1$

L: $-x - 3y + z = 1$
$-1 - 3(-1) + z = 1$
$-1 + 3 + z = 1$
$2 + z = 1 \Rightarrow z = -1$

$x = 1, y = -1, z = -1$

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: By comparing dimensions on both sides, we find x=1, y=-1, z=-1.

questions.question_text: If force $(F)$, velocity $(V)$ and time $(T)$ are taken as fundamental units, then the dimensions of mass are
questions.solution:
Let mass $m \propto F^a V^b T^c$

$m = k F^a V^b T^c$

Dimensions:
$[F] = [MLT^{-2}]$
$[V] = [LT^{-1}]$
$[T] = [T]$
$[m] = [M]$

$[M] = [MLT^{-2}]^a [LT^{-1}]^b [T]^c$

$[M] = [M^a L^{a+b} T^{-2a-b+c}]$

Comparing powers:

M: $a = 1$

L: $a + b = 0 \Rightarrow 1 + b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -1$

T: $-2a - b + c = 0$
$-2(1) - (-1) + c = 0$
$-2 + 1 + c = 0 \Rightarrow c = 1$

$[m] = [FV^{-1}T]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (d) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: When F, V and T are fundamental, mass has dimensions FV⁻¹T.

questions.question_text: The density of a material in CGS system of units is $4\text{ g cm}^{-3}$. In a system of units in which unit of length is $10\text{ cm}$ and unit of mass is $100\text{ g}$, the value of density of material will be
questions.solution:
Using the relation: $n_1 u_1 = n_2 u_2$

$4 \dfrac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3} = n_2 \dfrac{100\text{ g}}{(10\text{ cm})^3}$

$4 = n_2 \dfrac{100}{1000}$

$4 = n_2 \times 0.1$

$n_2 = 40$

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: When converting units, use the relation n₁u₁ = n₂u₂. The new density value is 40 in the new system.

questions.question_text: The velocity $v$ of a particle at time $t$ is given by $v = at + \dfrac{b}{t+c}$ where $a, b$ and $c$ are constants. The dimensions of $a, b$ and $c$ are
questions.solution:
$v = at + \dfrac{b}{t+c}$

Since $c$ is added to $t$, $[c] = [T]$

$[at] = [LT^{-1}] \Rightarrow [a] = \dfrac{[LT^{-1}]}{[T]} = [LT^{-2}]$

$\dfrac{[b]}{[T]} = [LT^{-1}] \Rightarrow [b] = [L]$

Therefore:
$[a] = [LT^{-2}]$
$[b] = [L]$
$[c] = [T]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (b) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Terms added must have same dimensions. The denominator (t+c) has dimension T, so c has dimension T.

questions.question_text: An equation is given here $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V^2}\right) = b\dfrac{\theta}{V}$ where $P =$ Pressure, $V =$ Volume and $\theta =$ Absolute temperature. If $a$ and $b$ are constants, the dimensions of $a$ will be
questions.solution:
Given: $\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V^2}\right) = b\dfrac{\theta}{V}$

Since $\dfrac{a}{V^2}$ is added to pressure $P$, both must have same dimensions.

$[a] = [P] \times [V^2]$

$[P] = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$
$[V^2] = [L^3]^2 = [L^6]$

$[a] = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}] \times [L^6]$

$[a] = [ML^5T^{-2}]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Since a/V² has same dimensions as pressure, a must have dimensions of pressure × V².

questions.question_text: Which of the following is a dimensional constant?
questions.solution:
A dimensional constant is a physical quantity that has dimensions and whose value is constant.

Relative density = dimensionless
Gravitational constant ($G$) = has dimensions $[M^{-1}L^3T^{-2}]$ = dimensional constant
Refractive index = dimensionless
Poisson's ratio = dimensionless

$\boxed{\text{Option (b) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Gravitational constant has dimensions and is constant. Relative density, refractive index, and Poisson's ratio are dimensionless.

questions.question_text: Turpentine oil is flowing through a tube of length $l$ and radius $r$. The pressure difference between the two ends of the tube is $P$. The viscosity of oil is given by $\eta = \dfrac{P(r^2 - x^2)}{4\nu l}$ where $\nu$ is the velocity of oil at a distance $x$ from the axis of the tube. The dimensions of $\eta$ are
questions.explanation: The formula is the standard expression for viscosity from Poiseuille's equation. Its dimensions are ML⁻¹T⁻¹.

questions.question_text: The time dependence of a physical quantity $p$ is given by $p = p_0 \exp(-\alpha t^2)$, where $\alpha$ is a constant and $t$ is the time. The constant $\alpha$
questions.solution:
$p = p_0 e^{-\alpha t^2}$

The argument of an exponential function must be dimensionless.

$\alpha t^2$ is dimensionless.

$[\alpha] = \dfrac{1}{[t^2]} = [T^{-2}]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (b) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: The exponent of e must be dimensionless. Since t² has dimension T², α must have dimension T⁻².

questions.question_text: $P$ represents radiation pressure, $c$ represents speed of light and $S$ represents radiation energy striking unit area per sec. The non zero integers $x, y, z$ such that $P^x S^y c^z$ is dimensionless are
questions.solution:
Let $k = P^x S^y c^z$, where $k$ is dimensionless.

Dimensions:
$[P] = \dfrac{\text{Force}}{\text{Area}} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$

$[S] = \dfrac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Area} \times \text{Time}} = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-2}]}{[L^2][T]} = [MT^{-3}]$

$[c] = [LT^{-1}]$

$[M^0L^0T^0] = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]^x [MT^{-3}]^y [LT^{-1}]^z$

Comparing powers of M, L, T:

M: $x + y = 0$

L: $-x + z = 0$

T: $-2x - 3y - z = 0$

Solving: $x = 1, y = -1, z = 1$

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: By dimensional analysis, P S⁻¹ c is dimensionless. So x=1, y=-1, z=1.

questions.question_text: The frequency of vibration $f$ of a mass $m$ suspended from a spring of spring constant $k$ is given by a relation $f = a m^x k^y$, where $a$ is a dimensionless constant. The values of $x$ and $y$ are
questions.solution:
$f = a m^x k^y$

Dimensions:
$[f] = [T^{-1}]$
$[m] = [M]$
$[k] = [MT^{-2}]$

$[T^{-1}] = [M]^x [MT^{-2}]^y$

$[T^{-1}] = [M^{x+y} T^{-2y}]$

Comparing powers of M and T:

M: $x + y = 0$

T: $-2y = -1 \Rightarrow y = \dfrac{1}{2}$

Therefore, $x = -\dfrac{1}{2}$

$x = -\dfrac{1}{2}, y = \dfrac{1}{2}$

$\boxed{\text{Option (d) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: The frequency of a spring-mass system is f = (1/2π)√(k/m), so x = -1/2 and y = 1/2.

questions.question_text: If $x = at + bt^2$, where $x$ is the distance travelled by the body in kilometers while $t$ is the time in seconds, then the units of $b$ is
questions.solution:
$x = at + bt^2$

Since $bt^2$ is added to $at$, both must have the same dimensions as $x$.

$[b] = \dfrac{[x]}{[t^2]}$

$[x] = [L] = \text{km}$
$[t^2] = [T^2] = \text{s}^2$

$[b] = \dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{s}^2}$

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Since bt² has dimensions of distance (km), b must have units of km/s².

questions.question_text:
A screw gauge gives the following readings when used to measure the diameter of a wire
Main scale reading : 0 mm
Circular scale reading : 52 divisions
Given that, 1 mm on main scale corresponds to 100 divisions on the circular scale. The diameter of the wire from the above data is
questions.solution:
Given:
MSR = 0 mm
CSR = 52 divisions
Pitch = 1 mm (since 1 mm corresponds to 100 divisions)

Least Count = $\dfrac{\text{Pitch}}{\text{Number of divisions}}$

$LC = \dfrac{1}{100} \text{ mm} = 0.01 \text{ mm}$

$LC = 0.001 \text{ cm}$

Diameter = MSR + CSR $\times$ LC

$D = 0 + 52 \times 0.001$

$D = 0.052 \text{ cm}$

$\boxed{\text{Option (d) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: The diameter is calculated using the formula: Diameter = MSR + (CSR × LC).

questions.question_text: If force $[F]$, acceleration $[a]$ and time $[T]$ are chosen as the fundamental physical quantities. Find the dimensions of energy.
questions.solution:
Energy depends on force, acceleration and time as:
$E \propto F^p a^q T^r$

$[E] = [ML^2T^{-2}]$
$[F] = [MLT^{-2}]$
$[a] = [LT^{-2}]$
$[T] = [T]$

$[ML^2T^{-2}] = [MLT^{-2}]^p [LT^{-2}]^q [T]^r$

$[ML^2T^{-2}] = [M^p L^{p+q} T^{-2p-2q+r}]$

Comparing powers of M, L, T:

M: $p = 1$

L: $p + q = 2 \Rightarrow 1 + q = 2 \Rightarrow q = 1$

T: $-2p - 2q + r = -2$
$-2(1) - 2(1) + r = -2$
$-2 - 2 + r = -2 \Rightarrow r = 2$

$[E] = [F^1 a^1 T^2] = [F a T^2]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (b) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Energy = mass × acceleration × distance = mass × (velocity²). In terms of F, a and T, it is F a T².

questions.question_text: If $E$ and $G$ respectively denote energy and gravitational constant, then $\dfrac{E}{G}$ has the dimensions of
questions.solution:
$[E] = [ML^2T^{-2}]$

$[G] = [M^{-1}L^3T^{-2}]$ (from Newton's law of gravitation)

$\left[\dfrac{E}{G}\right] = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-2}]}{[M^{-1}L^3T^{-2}]}$

$\left[\dfrac{E}{G}\right] = [M^{1-(-1)} L^{2-3} T^{-2-(-2)}]$

$\left[\dfrac{E}{G}\right] = [M^2L^{-1}T^0]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (a) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Energy divided by gravitational constant gives dimensions of M²L⁻¹.

questions.question_text: Plane angle and solid angle have :
questions.solution:
Plane angle (radian) and solid angle (steradian) are supplementary units in SI.

They are dimensionless quantities but have units.

Plane angle = arc length / radius → dimensionless
Solid angle = area / radius² → dimensionless

However, they have units: radian and steradian.

$\boxed{\text{Option (a) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Plane angle and solid angle are dimensionless but have units (radian and steradian respectively).

questions.question_text: The dimensions $[MLT^{-2}A^{-2}]$ belong to the:
questions.solution:
Dimensions of given quantities:

Magnetic flux = $[ML^2T^{-2}A^{-1}]$

Self inductance = $[ML^2T^{-2}A^{-2}]$

Magnetic permeability = $[MLT^{-2}A^{-2}]$

Electric permittivity = $[M^{-1}L^{-3}T^4A^2]$

Therefore, $[MLT^{-2}A^{-2}]$ belongs to magnetic permeability.

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Magnetic permeability (μ₀) has dimensions MLT⁻²A⁻².

questions.question_text: The area of a rectangular field (in m$^2$) of length 55.3 m and breadth 25 m after rounding off the value for correct significant digits is:
questions.solution:
Area = Length $\times$ Breadth

$= 55.3 \times 25$

$= 1382.5 \text{ m}^2$

Rule: In multiplication, the result should have the same number of significant figures as the quantity with the least significant figures.

55.3 has 3 significant figures.
25 has 2 significant figures.

Therefore, result should have 2 significant figures.

$1382.5 \rightarrow 1400 = 14 \times 10^2$

$\boxed{\text{Option (d) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: In multiplication, result has same number of significant figures as the least precise measurement. 25 has 2 significant figures.

questions.question_text: The errors in the measurement which arise due to unpredictable fluctuations in temperature and voltage supply are
questions.solution:
Errors due to unpredictable fluctuations in temperature and voltage supply are random errors.

Random errors occur due to unpredictable variations in experimental conditions.

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Random errors are caused by unpredictable fluctuations in experimental conditions like temperature, voltage, etc.

questions.question_text: A metal wire has mass (0.4 $\pm$ 0.002)g, radius (0.3 $\pm$ 0.001)mm and length (5 $\pm$ 0.02)cm. The maximum possible percentage error in the measurement of density will nearly be
questions.solution:
Density, $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{m}{\pi r^2 l}$

Maximum percentage error in density:

$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \times 100 = \left(\dfrac{\Delta m}{m} + 2\dfrac{\Delta r}{r} + \dfrac{\Delta l}{l}\right) \times 100$

Given:
$\Delta m = 0.002$ g, $m = 0.4$ g
$\Delta r = 0.001$ mm, $r = 0.3$ mm
$\Delta l = 0.02$ cm, $l = 5$ cm

$\dfrac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \times 100 = \left(\dfrac{0.002}{0.4} + 2\dfrac{0.001}{0.3} + \dfrac{0.02}{5}\right) \times 100$

$= (0.005 + 0.006667 + 0.004) \times 100$

$= 0.015667 \times 100$

$\approx 1.6\%$

$\boxed{\text{Option (b) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Density = mass/volume. Volume of wire = πr²l, so error in density = error in mass + 2×error in radius + error in length.

questions.question_text: In a vernier calipers, $(N + 1)$ divisions of vernier scale coincide with $N$ divisions of main scale. If 1 MSD represents 0.1 mm, the vernier constant (in cm) is:
questions.solution:
Given: $(N + 1)$ VSD = $N$ MSD

$1$ VSD = $\dfrac{N}{N+1}$ MSD

Vernier constant (LC) = 1 MSD $-$ 1 VSD

$LC = 1\text{ MSD} - \dfrac{N}{N+1}\text{ MSD}$

$LC = \dfrac{1}{N+1}\text{ MSD}$

1 MSD = 0.1 mm = $\dfrac{0.1}{10}$ cm = 0.01 cm

$LC = \dfrac{1}{N+1} \times 0.01$ cm

$LC = \dfrac{1}{100(N+1)}$ cm

$\boxed{\text{Option (d) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Vernier constant (least count) = 1 MSD / (Number of vernier divisions) = 1 MSD / (N+1).

questions.question_text: The quantities which have the same dimensions as those of solid angle are:
questions.solution:
Solid angle is a dimensionless quantity.

Strain = $\dfrac{\text{Change in length}}{\text{Original length}}$ = dimensionless

Angle = $\dfrac{\text{Arc length}}{\text{Radius}}$ = dimensionless

Therefore, strain and angle have the same dimensions as solid angle (dimensionless).

$\boxed{\text{Option (c) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: Solid angle, strain, and angle are all dimensionless quantities. Arc has dimension of length, angular speed has T⁻¹, and stress has ML⁻¹T⁻².

questions.question_text: A force defined by $F = \alpha t^2 + \beta t$ acts on a particle at a given time $t$. The factor which is dimensionless, if $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are constants, is:
questions.solution:
$F = \alpha t^2 + \beta t$

Since both terms represent force:

$[\alpha t^2] = [F] = [MLT^{-2}]$

$[\alpha] = \dfrac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[T^2]} = [MLT^{-4}]$

$[\beta t] = [F] = [MLT^{-2}]$

$[\beta] = \dfrac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[T]} = [MLT^{-3}]$

Now check each option for dimensionless:

$\left[\dfrac{\alpha t}{\beta}\right] = \dfrac{[MLT^{-4}] \times [T]}{[MLT^{-3}]} = [M^0L^0T^0]$

$\boxed{\text{Option (d) is correct}}$
questions.explanation: For a quantity to be dimensionless, all dimensions must cancel. Only αt/β is dimensionless.

questions.question_text: A balloon is made of a material of surface tension $S$ and its inflation outlet (from where gas is filled in it) has small area $A$. It is filled with a gas of density $\rho$ and takes a spherical shape of radius $R$. When the gas is allowed to flow freely out of it, its radius $r$ changes from $R$ to $0$ (zero) in time $T$. If the speed $v(r)$ of gas coming out of the balloon depends on $r$ as $r^a$ and $T \propto S^\alpha A^\beta \rho^\gamma R^\delta$ then
